posts.content: Very interested in what the coming era of highly bespoke software might look like. Example from this morning - I've become a bit loosy goosy with my cardio recently so I decided to do a more srs, regimented experiment to try to lower my Resting Heart Rate from 50 -> 45, over ...
posts.highlights: Karpathy explores personalized software for health experiments, reflecting on bespoke software trends.
posts.worthReading: Shows how AI can enable highly personalized, data-driven self-improvement tools.

posts.content: By training LLMs against automatically verifiable rewards across a number of environments (e.g. think math/code puzzles), the LLMs spontaneously develop strategies that look like 'reasoning' to humans - they learn to break down problem solving into intermediate calculations and they learn a number of problem-solving techniques.
posts.highlights: LLMs trained with verifiable rewards develop reasoning-like behaviors, breaking problems into intermediate steps.
posts.worthReading: Key insight into how reinforcement learning can elicit reasoning in LLMs without explicit programming.

posts.content: LLM Knowledge Bases Something I'm finding very useful recently: using LLMs to build personal knowledge bases for various topics of research interest. In this way, a large fraction of my recent token throughput is going less into manipulating code, and more into manipulating knowledge.
posts.highlights: Karpathy advocates using LLMs to create personal knowledge bases, shifting focus from code to knowledge management.
posts.worthReading: Highlights a practical application of LLMs for personal knowledge organization and research.

posts.content: I've never felt this much behind as a programmer. I have a sense that I could be 10X more powerful if I just properly string together what has become available.
posts.highlights: Karpathy expresses feeling behind as a programmer due to rapidly advancing AI tools, but sees potential for 10X productivity.
posts.worthReading: Reflects the challenge and opportunity of integrating modern AI tools into programming workflows.

posts.content: If you can’t stop small teams from using your API for distillation, then you’re definitely not stopping criminals, biohackers, or adversarial states from using AI through it. Those actors are much more sophisticated. The whole "APIs are safer" is a hoax!
posts.highlights: Clem Delangue argues that API-based safety claims are misleading because if small teams can bypass restrictions, sophisticated adversaries certainly can.
posts.worthReading: Challenges the narrative that APIs are inherently safer than open-source models.

posts.content: For those playing along at home, here's a "AI is sentient!" argument bingo card. Title: AI sentience/consciousness argument bingo Squares: You can’t prove it’s not conscious It told me it is What would convince you then? We should consider it, just in case we might be harming the AI Top minds have said so My conversation with GPT-3/LaMDA was just so impressive AIs have different brain architecture
posts.highlights: Bender critiques common arguments for AI sentience with a bingo card, highlighting the lack of rigorous evidence.
posts.worthReading: Shows Bender's skeptical, humorous take on AI hype and anthropomorphism.
